svg.text: NAHUATL (NĀHUATL) / ZAPOTEC (BENE ZAZA)
UTO-AZTECAN / OTO-MANGUEAN · MESOAMERICAN CIVILIZATIONS · ~260MI
PLATE 47A ALT · SCRIPT & SOUNDS
— NAHUATL: SCRIPT & WRITING —
Pre-conquest: pictographic / logographic glyphs in codices (screenfold books)
Post-conquest: Latin alphabet introduced by Spanish missionaries ~1540 CE
Classical Nahuatl uses Latin alphabet; modern dialects use modified orthographies
NAHUATL PHONOLOGY OVERVIEW
Consonants: /p t k kw ts tl tsh/ · /m n/ · /s sh/ · /l w j h/
Distinctive: /tl/ lateral affricate (as in Nahuatl, Quetzalcoatl, atl=water)
Vowels: /a e i o/ · vowel length phonemic: a vs ā (different meaning)
No tones · stress: penultimate syllable in most cases
THE LATERAL AFFRICATE /tl/
Nahuatl = /na.wa.tl/ · atl (water) = /atl/ · metl (agave) = /metl/
This /tl/ occurs word-finally (unique among world languages in this position)
Quetzalcoatl = /ket.sal.ko.atl/ — feathered serpent deity of the Aztec
POLYSYNTHETIC MORPHOLOGY
Nahuatl packs entire sentences into one word by stacking morphemes
Ni-tlā-pia = I-something-guard: "I am guarding something"
Ni-c-tlā-zo-tla = I-it-much-love: "I love it very much"
Incorporated nouns: ni-c-ā-tl-chia = I-it-water-see: "I look for water"
NAHUATL WORDS IN ENGLISH
chocolate (xocolatl) · tomato (tomatl) · avocado (ahuacatl)
chili (chilli) · coyote (coyotl) · mesquite (mizquitl)
tamale (tamalli) · ocelot (ocelotl) · guacamole (ahuaqa-molli)
SAMPLE WORDS
nāhutl = clear sound/speech (origin of name) · atl = water · tlalli = land
ZAPOTEC (BENE ZAZA) — OTO-MANGUEAN · OAXACA · ~260MI
— ZAPOTEC: SCRIPT & SOUNDS —
Pre-Columbian: Monte Alban glyphs (~500 BCE) — OLDEST writing in the Americas
Zapotec script: logographic/syllabic; partially deciphered; 50+ distinct symbols
Modern: Latin alphabet with diacritics; dozens of distinct Zapotec languages
ZAPOTEC IS A LANGUAGE COMPLEX: 50+ VARIETIES
Zapotec is not one language but a family of ~50 mutually unintelligible varieties
Valley Zapotec (Tlacolula) · Sierra Juarez · Isthmus Zapotec · Coastal Zapotec
Isthmus Zapotec (~70K speakers): most documented; used here as reference variety
ZAPOTEC PHONOLOGY: TONES AND LARYNGEALIZATION
4 tones: High · Low · Rising · Falling (in Isthmus Zapotec)
Laryngealization: breathy and creaky voice add further contrast
guela /ɡela/ (woman) vs guele’a /ɡeleʔa/ (vulture) — glottalization changes meaning
CONSONANTS
/p b t d k g/ · /ts dz/ · /m n/ · /s z sh/ · /l r/ · /j w/ · glottal /?/
Labio-velars: /kw gw/ · Voiced fricatives contrast with voiceless
SAMPLE WORDS (ISTHMUS ZAPOTEC)
bene = person · zaza = those · guela = woman · xhipe = man
neza = road/path · yoo = house · nisado = sea (nisa=water + do=big)
Zapotec = "people of the clouds" (Bene Za) or "people who eat beans" (folk etym.)
